questions.question: Which abolitionist, called 'Moses' by those she helped escape slavery, conducted 13 Underground Railroad missions and never lost a single passenger?
questions.explanation: Harriet Tubman (c. 1822-1913) escaped slavery in 1849 and returned 13 times to free approximately 70 enslaved people via the Underground Railroad. She was known as 'Moses' for leading her people to freedom. She later served as a spy and scout for the Union Army during the Civil War.
questions.memory_hook: Harriet Tubman = Moses. 13 missions, ~70 people freed. Never lost one.

questions.question: Which Argentine-born Marxist revolutionary, who fought alongside Fidel Castro in Cuba, later attempted to spread revolution across Africa and South America before being captured and executed in Bolivia in 1967?
questions.explanation: Ernesto 'Che' Guevara (1928-1967) was a physician-turned-revolutionary from Argentina who played a key role in the Cuban Revolution. After serving in Cuba's government, he left to foment revolution elsewhere. He was captured by Bolivian forces — aided by the CIA — and executed on October 9, 1967. His image became one of the most reproduced in history.
questions.memory_hook: Che Guevara: Argentine doctor, Cuba revolution, Bolivia execution 1967.

questions.question: The Reformation, which split Western Christianity, was ignited when which monk posted his 95 Theses in 1517?
questions.explanation: Martin Luther, a German monk and theology professor, nailed his 95 Theses to the door of the Castle Church in Wittenberg on October 31, 1517, protesting practices of the Roman Catholic Church, particularly the selling of indulgences. Thanks to Gutenberg's printing press, his ideas spread rapidly across Europe, sparking the Protestant Reformation and permanently splitting Western Christianity.
questions.memory_hook: Martin Luther, 95 Theses, 1517. The printing press made his ideas viral. Within weeks, copies were spread across Europe. One monk with a hammer and a printing press fractured Christianity permanently.

questions.question: The first successful transatlantic solo airplane flight was completed in 1927 by which aviator?
questions.explanation: Charles Lindbergh completed the first nonstop transatlantic solo flight on May 20-21, 1927, flying his Spirit of St. Louis from New York to Paris in 33.5 hours. He navigated without a radio or GPS by dead reckoning over the ocean. He was greeted by 100,000 people at Le Bourget airport in Paris. The flight made him an international celebrity almost overnight.
questions.memory_hook: Lindbergh, 1927, New York to Paris alone. 33.5 hours with no radio, no GPS, no autopilot. He stayed awake the entire flight by sticking his head out the window in cold air. 100,000 people met him in Paris.

questions.question: In which sport would athletes compete using a 'shuttlecock' — a feathered or plastic projectile — hit back and forth over a net?
questions.explanation: Badminton uses a shuttlecock (also called a birdie) — a cone-shaped projectile with 16 feathers attached. The sport originated in British India and was formalized in Poona (Pune). Professional shuttlecocks can travel at over 300 km/h (186 mph), making badminton one of the fastest racket sports.
questions.memory_hook: Badminton = shuttlecock. Can travel 300 km/h. Fastest racket sport.

questions.question: The Ryder Cup is a golf competition between players from which two teams?
questions.explanation: The Ryder Cup is a biennial team golf competition between the United States and Europe. It was first played in 1927 between the US and Great Britain. The European team was expanded to include all of Europe in 1979. The competition is unique in professional golf as players receive no prize money — they compete for national/continental pride. It is often described as the most passionate event in golf.
questions.memory_hook: Ryder Cup = USA vs Europe, every two years. No prize money — just pride. Players who earn millions on tour suddenly play for free, in front of screaming crowds, visibly emotional. Golf becomes a team sport.

questions.question: Which country's team is called the 'Springboks' in international rugby?
questions.explanation: South Africa's national rugby team is called the Springboks, named after the South African springbok antelope. The Springboks are one of the world's most successful rugby teams, winning the Rugby World Cup four times (1995, 2007, 2019, 2023). The 1995 World Cup victory, with Nelson Mandela presenting the trophy in a Springbok jersey, is one of sport's most powerful political moments.
questions.memory_hook: Springboks = South Africa. Nelson Mandela wore a Springbok jersey to present the 1995 World Cup trophy — previously a symbol of apartheid-era white South Africa. That moment of reconciliation became a symbol of the new South Africa. The film Invictus told the story.

questions.question: Which rapper's debut album 'Illmatic' (1994) is widely considered one of the greatest hip-hop albums ever recorded?
questions.explanation: Nas released Illmatic on April 19, 1994, at age 20. The album combined street poetry with jazz-inflected production and changed hip-hop forever. It was ranked 111th on Rolling Stone's 500 Greatest Albums of All Time. Despite being a commercial underperformer at launch, it is now considered the blueprint for East Coast hip-hop. Nas recorded it in his Queensbridge housing project apartment.
questions.memory_hook: Nas = Illmatic, 1994. Recorded at 20, in a housing project. Changed hip-hop forever. One of the most critically acclaimed debut albums in any genre. The critical consensus formed 20 years after release — it was ahead of its time.

questions.question: Which author, who has published over 60 novels and is often called 'the King of Horror,' wrote 'The Shining,' 'It,' 'Carrie,' and 'Misery'?
questions.explanation: Stephen King has sold over 350 million copies of his books worldwide. His works have been adapted into numerous films and TV series, including Stanley Kubrick's 'The Shining,' Tim Curry's 'It,' and Rob Reiner's 'Misery.' King is known for writing 2,000 words per day, even on holidays.
questions.memory_hook: Stephen King = King of Horror. The Shining, It, Carrie. 350M+ books sold.

questions.question: Which singer is known as the 'Material Girl' and released the album Like a Virgin in 1984?
questions.explanation: Madonna released Like a Virgin in November 1984, her second studio album. The title track reached number one in multiple countries. Her provocative performances and videos challenged social norms around female sexuality in pop music. 'Material Girl,' also from the album, gave her one of her enduring nicknames. Madonna has since become the best-selling female recording artist of all time.
questions.memory_hook: Madonna = Material Girl. Like a Virgin, 1984. She pushed every boundary the music industry and mainstream media had for women. Every generation of pop stars since owes something to Madonna breaking those barriers first.

questions.question: Which river is considered the longest in North America?
questions.explanation: The Missouri River is generally considered North America's longest river at approximately 3,767 km, slightly longer than the Mississippi (3,730 km). Together they form the Mississippi-Missouri river system, one of the longest in the world. The Missouri drains 1.37 million square kilometers and was a major route for westward expansion in 19th-century America — Lewis and Clark followed much of its course in 1804-1806.
questions.memory_hook: Missouri River = longest in North America. Most people say the Mississippi, but the Missouri is slightly longer. Together they form one of the world's great river systems — the Mississippi-Missouri drains most of the interior US.

questions.question: What is the term for animals that are active at night and sleep during the day?
questions.explanation: Nocturnal animals are active primarily at night. Bats, owls, raccoons, foxes, and most big cats are nocturnal. Many nocturnal animals have adaptations for low-light vision, such as larger pupils, a reflective layer behind the retina (tapetum lucidum), or highly sensitive whiskers. Some animals are crepuscular — most active at dawn and dusk — including deer and rabbits.
questions.memory_hook: Nocturnal = night active. The cat eyes glowing in the dark = tapetum lucidum, the reflective layer behind the retina that amplifies dim light. Owls see at light levels 100 times below what humans need. Evolution found a whole niche by just flipping the schedule.
